title: Predicting the Next Word
concept: N-gram language models predict the probability of the next word in a sequence based on the occurrences of the preceding N-1 words in a training text.

exercises.prompt: What does an N-gram model need first before it can start predicting the next word?

options.text: A large body of training text to count word patterns
An internet connection to search Google in real-time

exercises.explain: N-gram models rely on training text (past data) to learn which words frequently follow each other. Without text to analyze, they cannot calculate word probabilities.

cards.title: Mind-Reading Keyboards
cards.body: Have you ever noticed how your phone's keyboard tries to guess the next word you want to type? It feels like magic, but it is actually using math and patterns from your past messages to predict the future!

cards.title: What is an N-gram?
cards.body: An N-gram language model is a system that predicts the next word in a sentence by looking at the previous N-1 words. It counts how often words appear together in its training text to calculate which word is most likely to follow.

cards.title: The Phrase Predictor
cards.body: Imagine you are playing a word association game. If someone says "peanut butter and...", you immediately think "jelly" because you have heard those words together thousands of times. An N-gram model works the same way: it looks at the last few words and guesses the next word based on what usually comes next in its memory.

cards.title: Predicting the Next Word
cards.body: If a 2-gram (bigram) model reads the word "ice", it looks at its training data to see what usually follows. If "cream" appeared 80 times and "cube" appeared 20 times, the model will predict "cream" as the next word because it has a higher probability (80%).

cards.title: Do They Understand?
cards.body: Many people think language models understand the meaning of the words they predict. In reality, N-gram models don't understand concepts like 'delicious' or 'cold'; they just count which words have historically followed each other most frequently.

cards.title: Comparing N-gram Sizes
cards.body: The size of 'N' determines how much history the model considers before guessing the next word.

left.text: Looks back only 1 word. Fast, but lacks deep context (e.g., hears 'cream', predicts 'cone').

right.text: Looks back 2 words. More accurate because it has more context (e.g., hears 'sour cream', predicts 'onion').

exercises.prompt: When a bigram (2-gram) model predicts the next word, how much context does it look at?

options.text: The first word of the sentence

exercises.explain: A bigram model is a 2-gram model. It uses the previous N-1 words (2-1 = 1 word) to make its prediction. Therefore, it looks only at the single preceding word.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A 3-gram model generally makes more context-aware predictions than a 2-gram model.
exercises.explain: As "N" increases, the model looks at a larger history of words, which gives it more context to make a better, more coherent prediction.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how N-gram models rank potential next words.
exercises.sentence: An N-gram model calculates the [blank] of the next word based on historical patterns.
exercises.explain: N-gram models use math to calculate the probability (likelihood) of the next word based on how often it appeared in the training text.

exercises.prompt: Which of these systems is operating as an N-gram language model?

options.text: System A: A smart thermometer that predicts if it will rain by measuring air temperature.
System B: A text predictor that suggests 'birthday' after you type 'happy' because it saw that pair 500 times before.
System C: A search engine that lists web pages alphabetically.

exercises.explain: System B is using a simple statistical N-gram approach (counting past occurrences of word sequences), while System A uses a physical sensor. System C does not make predictions.

exercises.prompt: If you use a 3-gram model to write a long fantasy novel, why might the story start repeating itself or making no sense after a few sentences?

options.text: It only remembers the last few words and forgets the rest of the story.
It is programmed to hate writing long books.
It runs out of computer battery power too quickly.
It only knows how to write punctuation marks.

exercises.explain: An N-gram model only looks at a fixed window of the last N-1 words. If N=3 (a trigram model), it only looks at the 2 preceding words. It has no memory of what came before those 2 words, so it easily loses track of the overall story!
